serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of grilled salmon with garden salad, approximately 350 g, comprising a grilled salmon fillet (approximately 200–220 g) and a fresh garden salad (approximately 130–150 g) with dressing.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Salmon, Atlantic, farmed, cooked, dry heat (FDC ID 175168) combined with USDA FoodData Central — Salad, garden, with assorted vegetables (FDC ID 169977); weighted composite estimate at approximately 63% salmon and 37% salad by portion weight
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for grilled salmon with garden salad as a composite dish. Values derived from a weighted composite of USDA FDC ID 175168 (grilled Atlantic salmon, farmed) at approximately 63% of portion weight and USDA FDC ID 169977 (garden salad with assorted vegetables) at approximately 37% of portion weight, normalised to per-100g basis. Input record values (grabfood, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against this composite and found broadly consistent; USDA-derived composite adopted as the approved-source anchor. Sodium value reflects light dressing addition. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water set to -1 as detailed micronutrient breakdown for this specific composite preparation is not individually reported in a single approved-source entry.

gi_source.title: University of Sydney GI Database — no GI value listed for grilled salmon or grilled salmon with garden salad; GI not applicable for protein- and fat-dominant low-carbohydrate fish dish
gi_source.source_match_notes: Grilled salmon with garden salad contains approximately 3.8g carbohydrate per 100g, predominantly from salad vegetables. The carbohydrate content is too low and the food matrix too protein- and fat-dominant for a meaningful GI measurement. The University of Sydney GI Database does not list a GI value for grilled salmon or grilled salmon with salad. Per Veritas protocol for low-carbohydrate fish and seafood dishes, glycemic_index is set to null and gi_category to not_applicable. Glycemic load per serving is reported as 0.0 to reflect negligible glycemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (155 mg/100g) and high omega-3 fatty acid content from salmon supporting endothelial function
hypertension.guidance: Grilled salmon with garden salad is suitable for individuals with hypertension; sodium is low at approximately 155 mg/100g, well within DASH-diet targets, and the omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) in salmon support vascular endothelial function and modest blood pressure reduction — however, patients should request low-sodium dressing or plain olive oil and lemon to avoid hidden sodium from commercial vinaigrettes.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (3.8g/100g), high protein (13.2g/100g), and omega-3 fatty acids improving insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Grilled salmon with garden salad is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate content (~3.8g/100g), high-quality protein that supports satiety and glycaemic stability, and EPA/DHA omega-3 fatty acids that have been shown in clinical studies to improve insulin sensitivity — patients should specify sugar-free dressing to avoid added sugars from commercial vinaigrettes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids from Atlantic salmon with low saturated fat and anti-inflammatory salad components
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Grilled salmon with garden salad is strongly cardioprotective; Atlantic salmon provides EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids at clinically meaningful levels consistent with AHA recommendations of two servings of fatty fish per week, saturated fat is low at approximately 1.6g/100g, and the garden salad contributes dietary fibre, potassium, and antioxidant polyphenols — patients with established CVD should avoid high-sodium commercial dressings and ensure the dish is grilled without butter.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids from salmon directly modulating prostaglandin and leukotriene inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Grilled salmon is one of the most beneficial foods for rheumatoid arthritis due to its high EPA and DHA content, which directly reduces joint inflammation; individuals with nightshade sensitivity should monitor response to tomato in the salad, and vinaigrette should be prepared with olive oil rather than pro-inflammatory seed oils.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled Salmon with Garden Salad is a nutritionally dense, protein-rich dish consisting of a grilled Atlantic salmon fillet served alongside a fresh garden salad of mixed greens, lettuce, tomato, and cucumber, typically dressed with a light vinaigrette or olive oil and lemon juice. The salmon is cooked using dry-heat grilling, which preserves its natural omega-3 fatty acid content (EPA and DHA) while producing a lightly charred, firm exterior and moist, flaky interior with a rich, savoury flavour. The dish is commonly served in Western-style and international restaurants across Singapore, including Indian fusion restaurants that incorporate Western seafood dishes into their menus. Per 100g, the dish provides approximately 131 kcal, 13.2g protein, 7.1g fat (predominantly unsaturated), and only 3.8g carbohydrate, making it one of the lowest-carbohydrate restaurant main courses available. Clinically, this dish is highly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease due to its low glycaemic impact, low sodium, low saturated fat, and high omega-3 fatty acid content. The omega-3 fatty acids in Atlantic salmon are among the highest of any commonly consumed food and are associated with reduced triglycerides, improved endothelial function, reduced joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis, and improved insulin sensitivity. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. A standard restaurant serving is approximately 350g (1 plate), providing approximately 459 kcal, 46g protein, and 25g fat per serving.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for grilled salmon with garden salad as a composite dish. Nutrient values are derived from a weighted composite of two USDA FoodData Central entries (grilled Atlantic salmon FDC 175168 and garden salad FDC 169977) normalised to per-100g basis. The composite approach is methodologically sound but introduces estimation uncertainty, particularly for micronutrients. Input record values (grabfood, confidence 0.7) were broadly consistent with the USDA composite and served as a cross-check. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are a weighted composite estimate derived from USDA FDC ID 175168 (grilled Atlantic salmon, farmed) at approximately 63% of portion weight and USDA FDC ID 169977 (garden salad with assorted vegetables) at approximately 37% of portion weight, normalised to per-100g. No single approved-source entry covers this composite dish. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient breakdown for this specific composite preparation (grilled salmon + garden salad) is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024; individual component values exist in USDA FDC but were not disaggregated to avoid compounding estimation error across multiple fields. 3. Glycemic index is set to null and gi_category to not_applicable: grilled salmon with garden salad contains approximately 3.8g carbohydrate per 100g, predominantly from salad vegetables; the food matrix is protein- and fat-dominant and does not produce a meaningful GI response; the University of Sydney GI Database does not list a GI value for this dish or its dominant component (grilled salmon). 4. Input record cultural tags ('Indian', 'Chicken Dish') were corrected: the dish is a Western/international seafood dish served in a Singapore restaurant context; ethnic_tags have been updated accordingly. 5. The input record's grabfood-sourced nutrient values (confidence 0.7) were retained as a cross-check reference but replaced with USDA-derived composite values as the approved-source anchor per Project Veritas source policy. 6. Trans fat is reported as 0.03g/100g consistent with the input record; this trace level is consistent with naturally occurring trans fats in fish and is not a formulation addition. 7. Halal status is marked true as salmon is permissible under Islamic dietary law; kosher status is marked false as the dish combines fish with potential non-kosher salad dressing ingredients and cannot be confirmed kosher without preparation verification. 8. Ontology match types corrected to 'closest_parent' for both FoodOn and FoodEx2 per the allowed enumeration values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none']; 'broader' and 'adapted' are not valid match_type values in the scoring model.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index is null (not_applicable for low-carbohydrate fish dish per Veritas protocol for negligible-carbohydrate foods), gi_category is 'not_applicable', glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0, and gi_source is fully documented referencing the University of Sydney GI Database with rationale. Setting glycemic_index to null rather than 0 is correct per the q4 critical rule.
